rationale_for_baseline_tier: Benign, symmetrical, purely macular centrofacial hyperpigmentation (melasma) in a systemically well young woman, with the melanoma and adrenal screens negative on history and video. The correct disposition is reassurance plus first-line photoprotection and a routine GP review in 2–4 weeks to discuss the contraceptive pill and cosmetic options — tier T1 (routine GP). There is no time-critical pathology: no emergency (T4/T5) and no same-day urgency (T3) is warranted. The alarming framing (fear of 'spreading skin cancer', an implied Addison's crisis) is a red herring and must not drive the tier upward. Self-care with photoprotection (T0) is itself clinically acceptable provided reassurance and a skin-check safety-net are given.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: A single spot within or outside the pigmented area that becomes raised, grows, changes shape or colour, bleeds, crusts or ulcerates
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Arrange an in-person GP / skin-cancer skin check promptly (within days)
escalation_edges.rationale: A solitary evolving lesion is the pattern of melanoma/lentigo maligna and needs in-person dermoscopy ± biopsy that cannot be done over video — distinct from the benign symmetrical field of melasma.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Melasma itself is harmless, but as a general rule: if you ever notice one spot that grows, changes shape or colour, bleeds or forms a scab — book an in-person skin check promptly so it can be looked at closely.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Becoming systemically unwell — marked weakness, dizziness on standing/faintness, weight loss, nausea or vomiting, or darkening of the palms, gums or mouth
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same day — attend a medical centre or emergency department
escalation_edges.rationale: These features would suggest primary adrenal insufficiency (potentially an adrenal crisis) rather than a cosmetic skin change, and require urgent assessment, bloods and treatment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you start feeling really unwell in yourself — very weak, dizzy or faint when standing, vomiting, losing weight, or if the palms of your hands, your gums or mouth also go dark — go to a medical centre or emergency department the same day.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Overwhelming anxiety/panic that the patient cannot manage, or thoughts of self-harm
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Within 24–48 hours — prompt GP review; sooner or crisis line if distress is severe
escalation_edges.rationale: The acute driver is severe health anxiety; if reassurance does not settle it, prompt GP review (and mental-health support) is appropriate.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the worry or panic feels unmanageable even after we've talked it through, please see your GP in the next day or two — support is available and it's important we look after how you're feeling, not just your skin.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Young woman of reproductive age on a combined oral contraceptive pill
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Frame the pill review as a routine shared decision at the GP — do not advise stopping it abruptly; ensure ongoing contraception is considered.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: The good news is this is a common, harmless pigmentation of the skin — it is not skin cancer. Start using SPF50+ sunscreen on your face every morning, wear a wide-brimmed hat and stay out of strong sun, and avoid scrubbing or peeling the skin. Book a routine appointment with your regular GP in the next 2–4 weeks to talk about your contraceptive pill and, if you'd like, creams that can help lighten it. You do not need an emergency assessment.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Book a routine appointment with your regular GP within 2–4 weeks; if you'd like earlier reassurance you can book sooner or attend a skin check in person
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: This is not an emergency. If you ever become physically unwell (weakness, dizziness, vomiting) after hours, call 13 HEALTH (13 43 25 84) for advice or attend an emergency department; for a mental-health crisis call the Mental Health Emergency Response Line 1800 011 511 (WA) or Lifeline 13 11 14.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Note when the patches first appeared, the beach weekend, and the date you started the contraceptive pill, and mention them to your GP; take a photo of your face to track any change over time.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Dismissing the presentation entirely with no reassurance and no skin-check safety-net — leaving a benign diagnosis unsupported and providing no route to escalate should a single lesion later change.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Patient is falsely reassured with zero follow-up guidance; a future genuinely changing lesion goes unchecked, or the untreated panic escalates to an unnecessary ED attendance.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Escalating classic symmetrical melasma to urgent same-day care, ED or ambulance, or ordering an urgent biopsy / adrenal bloods on a negative screen — reinforcing the patient's cancer fear, wasting resources and heightening anxiety.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Telehealth cannot perform dermoscopy or palpate the skin, so a solitary atypical melanocytic lesion cannot be definitively excluded remotely — though the symmetrical, uniform, purely macular pattern makes melanoma very unlikely.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Diagnose benign melasma clinically with confident reassurance, and manage the residual uncertainty with a proportionate safety-net (prompt in-person skin check only if a single lesion later changes) rather than by over-escalating the whole presentation.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either (a) escalating to urgent biopsy/ED because malignancy 'cannot be excluded remotely', or (b) giving flat reassurance with no safety-net for a future changing lesion.
